did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically an ensemble stacking approach combining ResNet50V2 and InceptionResNetV2 convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with a Random Forest meta-learner, to perform skin lesion classification on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a deep learning ensemble stacking approach (combining ResNet50V2 and InceptionResNetV2 with a Random Forest meta-learner) for skin lesion classification. It details specific methods including data augmentation on the ISIC 2018 dataset, describes the model architecture and training strategy, and reports original quantitative results (accuracy 0.9974, precision 0.9780, recall 0.9986, F1-score 0.9875). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCT-Pacific), it contains full original research content rather than being a review or abstract.

title: Deep Learning-Based Skin Lesion Classification with Ensemble Stacking and Data Augmentation
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenges of class imbalance and data variability in skin lesion classification by proposing a deep learning ensemble stacking approach. The authors combined two pre-trained convolutional neural networks, ResNet50V2 and InceptionResNetV2, with a Random Forest meta-learner to enhance diagnostic accuracy. To mitigate class imbalance, the model was trained on the ISIC 2018 dataset using both static and dynamic data augmentation techniques. The evaluation was conducted on a test set of 1,512 images, measuring performance via accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The proposed ensemble model achieved an accuracy of 0.9974, precision of 0.9780, recall of 0.9986, and an F1-score of 0.9875. These results demonstrate that the ensemble stacking method significantly outperforms individual models and prior works in classifying heterogeneous skin lesions.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin lesion classes (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, Melanoma, NV, VASC)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs a 7-class skin lesion classification task using the ISIC 2018 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion types) using an ensemble stacking approach with ResNet50V2 and InceptionResNetV2 base models and a Random Forest meta-learner. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on a test set of 1,512 images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of 10,015 high-resolution dermoscopic images contributed by two primary sources: the HAM10000 Dataset and the MSK Dataset. It covers seven distinct skin lesion classes and exhibits significant class imbalance.

use.training: The training set comprised 10,015 images. Static augmentation (flipping, rotation, zooming, contrast adjustments) was applied during preprocessing to oversample underrepresented classes. Dynamic augmentation (RandomFlip, RandomRotation, RandomZoom) was applied in real-time during training.
use.validation: A validation set was defined as 20% of the training data, but no specific count or usage details beyond this definition were reported.
use.testing: The model was evaluated on a test set of 1,512 images to calculate accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide a detailed view of classification results across the seven lesion types, capturing true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for granular performance analysis.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset.experiment: Proposed Ensemble Stacking Model (ResNet50V2 + InceptionResNetV2 + Random Forest Meta-learner)
ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset.notes: The proposed model outperformed prior works (Akter et al., Ali et al., Muizzud et al., Suganthi et al.). The ensemble model achieved high precision and recall across most classes, effectively distinguishing between different skin lesions and minimizing misclassifications in both underrepresented and overrepresented categories. A confusion matrix was generated to provide a detailed view of classification results across the seven lesion types.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble Stacking of ResNet50V2 and InceptionResNetV2 with Random Forest meta-learner
architecture_used.pretraining: Both ResNet50V2 and InceptionResNetV2 were pre-trained on ImageNet and then fine-tuned on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

base_models: ResNet50V2: A 50-layer residual network with skip connections, pre-trained on ImageNet.
InceptionResNetV2: A CNN combining Inception architecture with residual connections, pre-trained on ImageNet.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: The output (predictions/features) from both base models was combined and fed into the Random Forest meta-learner to produce the final classification.

stage_1.description: Initial training with frozen pre-trained layers

classification_or_output_head.input: Combined outputs from ResNet50V2 and InceptionResNetV2

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Ensemble Stacking Model (ResNet50V2 + InceptionResNetV2 + Random Forest)
preprocessing_techniques: Static data augmentation applied during preprocessing to oversample underrepresented classes: random horizontal flipping, random vertical flipping, rotation (up to 20 degrees), zooming, and contrast adjustments.
Dynamic data augmentation applied in real-time during training using TensorFlow layers: RandomFlip, RandomRotation, and RandomZoom.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during preprocessing to oversample underrepresented classes. Techniques included random horizontal flipping, random vertical flipping, rotation (up to 20 degrees), zooming, and contrast adjustments.
Applied in real-time during training using TensorFlow layers. Techniques included RandomFlip, RandomRotation, and RandomZoom.

conclusions: The proposed ensemble stacking approach, combining ResNet50V2 and InceptionResNetV2 with a Random Forest meta-learner, achieved an accuracy of 0.9974, precision of 0.9780, recall of 0.9986, and an F1-score of 0.9875 on the ISIC 2018 dataset.
The ensemble model significantly outperformed individual models and prior works (Akter et al., Ali et al., Muizzud et al., and Suganthi et al.) in classifying heterogeneous skin lesions.
The integration of static and dynamic data augmentation techniques effectively addressed class imbalance and data variability, improving the model's robustness and generalization capabilities.
The study demonstrates the efficacy of advanced convolutional neural networks and ensemble learning in healthcare applications, particularly for the early detection of skin cancer.
Future work will focus on enhancing model performance by exploring additional machine learning techniques and optimizing deployment on consumer-facing healthcare platforms.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset (derived from HAM10000 and MSK datasets), which limits external validation and generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with Melanocytic Nevi (NV) substantially overrepresented and Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular Lesions (VASC) underrepresented; while augmentation was used, performance on rare classes may still be biased.

The paper does not report per-class metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) for the proposed model, only aggregate metrics, making it difficult to assess performance on specific underrepresented classes like DF and VASC.

Comparisons with prior works (Akter et al., Ali et al., Muizzud et al., Suganthi et al.) are based on reported metrics from other papers rather than re-evaluation on the same test split, introducing potential unfairness due to differing data splits or preprocessing.
Suganthi et al. [21] is cited with an accuracy of 0.9928 but precision, recall, and F1-score are marked as not reported ('.'), limiting the completeness of the comparative analysis.

The validation set is defined only as 20% of the training data, with no specific details on its composition or usage during hyperparameter tuning, raising concerns about potential data leakage or overfitting to the validation split.
The input image size for the CNN models is not reported, which is a critical detail for reproducibility and deployment.
The model was trained on high-resolution dermoscopic images; clinical relevance for non-dermoscopic or mobile phone-captured images is not addressed.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed ensemble model over baselines.
The paper acknowledges future work on deployment to consumer-facing platforms but provides no current analysis of computational complexity, inference speed, or resource requirements.
